fund.name: Franklin India SHORT TERM INCOME PLAN - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: You want short-duration debt with minimal volatility. This is a very conservative debt fund that keeps money in very short-term papers (like overnight and repos). Think of it as your 'pocket savings' in the debt world—very safe, low risk, and you can see the market interest rate risk clearly because the term is so short. No surprises.

fund.name: BANK OF INDIA Credit Risk Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: You are okay with a tiny bit more risk for a slightly better return, but only if you know what you are buying. This fund invests in banks' risky bonds (Credit Risk). It is not for you if you want zero risk, but if you like investing in banks, this lets you own the market’s high-yield bonds directly. It is a 'riskier' debt fund, so we keep the allocation low (20%) to keep the whole portfolio very safe.

You already have gold in your wardrobe and assets. This is an easy, low-cost way to add more gold to your portfolio without buying physical bars every month. Gold prices go up and down just like other things, but over 13 years it usually keeps pace with inflation and acts as a safety net when the stock market goes down. It is simple and direct.

fund.name: quant Multi Asset Allocation Fund-GROWTH OPTION-Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This is your main 'working' fund that has a bit more risk designed to generate growth. It splits your money between stocks and debt automatically (and you can see exactly how it is doing). It uses a unique 'arbitrage' method which means it often hedges risk in the stock market, making it safer than a normal equity fund. It is the most balanced option that still aims to beat inflation significantly over 13 years, while keeping your 'no unknown stocks' fear in check.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on any app (Zerodha, Groww, Coin, Kuvera). You will need your PAN, cancelled cheque, and ID proof. Step 2: Select each fund by its name or AMFI code listed above. Step 3: Set up the SIPs for ₹20,000, ₹16,000, ₹16,000, and ₹28,000 respectively. Step 4: Do not try to move your existing ₹120 lakh lump-sum here yet. We will keep that as a separate pot for now to let the SIPs build up a fresh portfolio.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that all 4 SIPs are running and money is debiting your account correctly. Make sure you get the monthly statements from each fund. Step 2 is to ensure the money is actually going where you think it is—no 'mystery funds'.

After 1 year: Look at each fund’s performance vs its benchmark. If one of the 20% credit risk fund or the 20% gold ETF has lost value significantly (e.g., down 10% in a year), ask yourself if you still feel comfortable holding it. If the gold fund looks like it is underperforming its peers by too much, just swap it for a different Gold ETF. For the debt funds, if interest rates fall, remember your short-term debt fund will naturally do better as rates rise, but if rates have already fallen, it is still safe.

Every 6 months: Rebalance the portfolio. If Gold has gone up and now makes up 25% of your portfolio instead of 20%, sell some gold units and buy more of the other parts to get back to your original 25/20/20/35 split. This keeps risk in check.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once every 6 months, check the total allocation of each category. If any part has moved by more than 5 percentage points from your target (25-20-20-35), move money from the 'winner' part to the 'loser' part to bring it back to target. For example, if Gold is now 30% of your portfolio, sell 5% of Gold and buy the 25% Credit Risk Fund (if it is underweight) or the Multi Asset Fund. This is important because if Gold goes up too much, it becomes too risky. If the debt funds drop too much, it becomes too safe.
plan.rationale: At 47 with a 13-year horizon, you are in a 'sweet spot'. You don't have enough time to let a risky stock market crash drag you down to zero (like a 20-year-old would), but you also have enough time for the money to work for you. Your existing land and gold wealth shows you are naturally conservative, but you need a little engine to generate the ₹1 lac/month you want by 60. Our plan does exactly that: 35% in a smart 'Multi-Asset' fund that mixes stocks and debt, 55% in debt (short duration + credit risk) to keep the plan stable. We stay out of fancy 'Opportunity' funds and stick to simple, transparent instruments. Since you are in the 30% tax bracket, we have used Direct plans to avoid the extra yearly fees and bought debt funds that are sensitive to inflation. We have not used equity-taxed funds with >10% equity for you because you are too conservative, but the Multi-Asset fund is a must-have because it gives you some stock upside while keeping volatility in check.
risks: Debt Risk: Your ₹20,000 in Credit Risk funds will go down if banks stop lending or if the economy slows. It is a small part (20%) so it won't hurt you badly. Stick to banking bonds for now; if you see headlines about banks, stop adding money here.

Stock/Debt Balance Risk: The Multi-Asset fund (35%) will go up and down a bit because it holds stocks. It is not as stable as a pure debt fund. If it drops 20% in a year, don't panic—stick to the plan. It is a small part of your big edifice.

Gold Risk: Gold can be very quiet and flat for long periods. If gold stays flat for 3 years, you will feel like you are losing ground even if everything else goes up. But remember, gold is your 'safety net'—it helps when other things crash.

Time Horizon Risk: 13 years is not 'forever'. If you need this money earlier (e.g., for your son's marriage), you will have to pull out of the plan. That is why we kept it balanced so you could use the money earlier if needed.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total monthly SIP by 10% every year (approx ₹8,000 extra in SIP each year) to beat inflation and grow your corpus faster as your income grows.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The plan allocates 35% to a multi-asset fund (which may have equity exposure), but there is no indication that the equity portion is invested in index funds or ETFs. The rationale emphasizes 'arbitrage' and active risk management, suggesting active equity strategies. Since no explicit passive/index equity instruments are used, the score is 0.0.

turnover_score: The plan mandates rebalancing every 6 months if allocations deviate by more than 5 percentage points, and includes semi-annual review checkpoints. This indicates moderate turnover — not monthly, but more frequent than annual. Hence, a score of 0.33 (closer to active due to systematic 6-month rebalancing).

cost_emphasis_score: The plan mentions using Direct plans to avoid extra fees and references tax efficiency due to the investor's 30% tax bracket. However, expense ratios are not discussed in fund selection, and cost is not the primary criterion — safety and simplicity are. Thus, cost is considered important but not dominant, leading to a 0.6.

research_vs_cost_score: The fund choices are based on risk profile, simplicity, and transparency (e.g., avoiding 'fancy Opportunity funds'), but there's no evidence of deep stock or fund research (e.g., no mention of manager track record, P/E ratios, or company analysis). However, the selection rationale focuses more on behavioral alignment and risk control than broad market exposure, so it leans active by intent. Score is 0.0 as it does not adopt a 'no research, broad market' philosophy.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 13-year horizon with SIPs and annual step-ups, and the rationale acknowledges long-term compounding. Review checkpoints are regular but not short-term speculative. Hence, it aligns well with a long-term view, scoring 0.8 (not 1.0 due to semi-annual rebalancing).

portfolio_activeness_score: The portfolio is 85% debt and 20% gold, with 35% in a multi-asset fund that uses 'arbitrage' strategies — typically actively managed. No risk metrics are provided, but the rationale describes active risk management and hedging. The debt and gold components are actively managed (credit risk fund, ETF). Given the emphasis on active strategies and lack of passive indexing, the portfolio is assessed as actively managed, scoring 0.2 (nearly active, but ETF and direct plans add slight passivity).

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows strong alignment with Ron Swanson's conservative risk appetite, life stage, and goals. It avoids complex equity funds and respects his distrust of financial institutions by using transparent, simple instruments like short-duration debt, gold ETF, and a multi-asset fund with arbitrage-based equity exposure. The 13-year horizon is respected, and the projected returns (8.6% base) are reasonable for the allocation. Diversification is adequate across debt (short-term and credit risk), gold, and a multi-asset fund, though the equity portion is indirect and concentrated in one multi-asset fund, limiting broad market diversification. The plan is internally consistent: the rationale, fund choices, rebalancing, and risk disclosures align well, with clear review checkpoints and rebalancing rules. However, tax efficiency is poor. The investor is in the 30% tax slab, yet the plan uses slab-taxed debt funds (Short Term Income Plan, Credit Risk Fund) instead of equity-taxed hybrids like Arbitrage or Aggressive Hybrid funds, which would offer similar risk profiles but with more favorable LTCG treatment. The plan incorrectly claims to avoid equity-taxed funds 'because you are too conservative', but fails to recognize that Arbitrage funds are low-volatility and equity-taxed — ideal for high-tax-bracket conservative investors. ELSS is not included, which is acceptable only if the investor is under the new regime, but here the tax_bracket is '30_percent_slab' (old regime), so 80C headroom should be used. The plan misses both tax optimization levers: equity taxation via arbitrage/hybrids and 80C via ELSS. This results in a significant drag on post-tax returns, making the tax_efficiency score low.
